summary: In this advice column, Cathy O'Neil (as Aunt Pythia) addresses questions from readers about career choices, mathematical thinking, social etiquette, and data journalism. She strongly advises an MIT math student against pursuing finance for money while ignoring the real-world impacts of their work. She discusses how mathematicians visualize problems, offers perspective on asking about ethnic backgrounds with good intentions, and reflects on bias in data journalism. The column emphasizes that transparency about bias is more achievable than objectivity, and that the ethics of applied mathematics deserve serious consideration.
key_points: Pursuing pure math you love is more valuable than taking courses for 'cred' with financial companies - you can learn practical skills later
Once you leave pure math, your work has real-world consequences that affect real people, so consider carefully who you work for
Most mathematicians think visually, though not all, and even geometers often lack good 3D spatial sense or sense of direction
True objectivity in data journalism is impossible because bias exists at minimum in how data is collected - transparency about bias is the realistic goal
Asking about someone's background is fine if your intention is friendly connection rather than hostility
money_quotes: Don't take the math to get 'cred' with financial companies. Do what is sexy and beautiful to you.
Once you leave pure math there are real applications of your work, and they affect real people. Shit gets real real quick and stuff matters.
We will never have unbiased accounts. Never. At the very least we will have bias in the way that data is collected.
If your intention is to be mean, then don't ask it. If your intention is to be friendly and to make a connection, then go ahead and ask it!
key_insight: Mathematical work has real-world consequences once it leaves the realm of pure theory, so practitioners must think critically about who benefits and who is harmed by their contributions.
